entries.title: Missing Invariant Definitions
entries.summary: Value flows described without stating properties that must always hold

indicators: Token flows without conservation invariants
Fee mechanisms without constraint specifications
No explicit statement of what must always be true
design_context.description: The design describes value flows but doesn't state the properties that must always hold. Without explicit invariants, there's no way to verify correctness or detect violations.

entries.title: Unanalyzed Extraction Vectors
entries.summary: No consideration of whether attacker can extract more value than contributed

indicators: Share-based accounting without first-depositor protection
Price-sensitive operations without slippage bounds
No mention of MEV, frontrunning, or flash loan scenarios
design_context.description: The design doesn't consider whether an attacker can extract more value than they contribute. Flash loans, sandwich attacks, and donation attacks are not addressed.

entries.title: Incentive Misalignment
entries.summary: Design creates incentives rewarding behavior contrary to protocol goals

indicators: Reward mechanisms that can be gamed by rational actors
Governance votes purchasable cheaply
Liquidation incentives that create cascading failures
design_context.description: The design creates incentives that reward behavior contrary to protocol goals. Rational actors deviate from intended usage because deviation is more profitable.

entries.title: Fee or split parameters read at claim time rather than accrual time — changes retroactively affect previously earned value
entries.summary: When fee/split/share parameters are resolved at claim time instead of accrual time, governance changes to these parameters retroactively affect all previously accrued but unclaimed value.

indicators: Fee, split, or share percentage queried from external contract at claim time
No snapshotting or checkpointing of parameter values at accrual time
Governance can change the parameter between accrual and claim
No time-weighted average or historical parameter tracking
design_context.description: Designs that resolve fee/split parameters at claim time rather than accrual time create a retroactivity problem. If a fee split increases from 0% to 5% between when value is earned and when it's claimed, the user loses 5% of previously-earned value to a split recipient that didn't exist when the value was accrued. The fix is to snapshot parameters at accrual time or use time-weighted averaging.

entries.auditor_notes: Confirmed. Impact bounded by hard 5% cap but pattern is generalizable.

confirmed_by.context: Confirmed in design review — fee split retroactivity

entries.title: Zero-value operations that modify global state can be used as cost-free tools for adversarial state manipulation
entries.summary: Operations that succeed with zero value but still modify global state (advancing accumulators, settling indexes) become cost-free tools for adversarial manipulation.

indicators: Operations succeed when the computed value is zero (no early return or skip)
Zero-value operations still execute state-modifying side effects
No gas cost or economic stake required beyond transaction fees
Settlement timing affects value distribution for other users
design_context.description: When a design allows zero-value operations to proceed and modify global state, these operations become free tools for adversaries. Zero-value operations that advance settlement state let attackers control when settlement happens for all users on a component, enabling timing attacks, front-running coordination, and compound attack chains.

entries.auditor_notes: Confirmed. Key enabler in two compound attack chains.

confirmed_by.context: Confirmed in design review — zero-value operations as settlement manipulation tool

entries.title: Mixing block-based and timestamp-based units for related mechanisms creates cross-chain inconsistency
entries.summary: When one mechanism uses block counts and a related mechanism uses timestamps, the relationship between them varies across chains with different block times.

indicators: One mechanism uses block numbers/counts while a related mechanism uses timestamps
Multi-chain deployment with varying block times
No per-chain calibration specified for the block-based parameter
The block-based mechanism gates or modifies the timestamp-based mechanism
design_context.description: Designs that mix temporal units (blocks vs timestamps) for related mechanisms create implicit cross-chain inconsistency. A 1000-block penalty window is ~3.3 hours on Ethereum (12s blocks) but ~4 minutes on Arbitrum (0.25s blocks). If the timestamp-based mechanism interacts with the block-based mechanism, the interaction behaves differently on each chain without explicit per-chain calibration.

entries.auditor_notes: Confirmed. Impact bounded to penalty effectiveness, not direct fund loss.

confirmed_by.context: Confirmed in design review — block-based penalty vs timestamp-based accrual

entries.title: Auxiliary mint path (fee, share, splitter) bypasses the main solvency cap, making total supply growth unbounded by the stated invariant
entries.summary: The design presents a solvency cap (ceiling, allocation, max supply) as the core constraint on minting. A secondary mint path -- typically for fees, team shares, or splitter recipients -- is not bounded by the same cap. A misconfigured or compromised parameter on the secondary path produces unbounded inflation while the stated invariant still holds.

indicators: Primary mint path is bounded by an explicit cap (ceiling, max-supply, allocation)
Secondary mint path (fee share, team split, referral bonus, emission multiplier) mints in addition to the primary amount
The secondary mint is controlled by a parameter on a different contract than where the cap is enforced
No stated bound, timelock, or solvency check on the secondary-path parameter
The cap's calling contract does not know the split percentage or the absolute secondary amount
design_context.description: When the solvency-bearing contract passes an amount X to a minter that then mints X + f(X) -- where f(X) is determined by an external parameter -- the cap only constrains X, not total inflation. If f is governed by a parameter with no bound or timelock, a misconfiguration or compromise of that parameter produces arbitrary inflation. The stated invariant (primary mint <= cap) still holds, but 'total minted <= cap' does not. This pattern frequently appears in fee-share, team-rate, referral-bonus, and splitter-on-top-of-mint mechanisms.

entries.auditor_notes: Confirmed. Team will bound the secondary-path parameter to mitigate.

confirmed_by.context: Confirmed in cross-chain emission-accounting design review

entries.title: Monotonic safety accumulator with no correction mechanism -- any overcount from rounding, concurrency, or ordering becomes permanent tightening
entries.summary: A monotonic accumulator (only increases) used in a safety check has no reverse or correction path. Any overcount -- from rounding, concurrent operations, out-of-order message arrival, or missing state resets -- permanently tightens downstream checks with no way to recover except through external intervention.

indicators: Accumulator is explicitly documented as monotonic or 'only increases'
Accumulator feeds a safety/solvency check (check uses accumulator as a consumed-bound)
No correction, rollback, or decrement mechanism specified
Multiple sources feed the accumulator (different triggers, cross-chain messages, per-component reports)
Design assumes 'undercount is safe' without verifying that overcount is impossible
design_context.description: Monotonic accumulators are often chosen to simplify safety reasoning -- the system is assumed safe if the accumulator can only grow. But an overcount from rounding, concurrent writes, cross-chain message ordering, or a missed state reset permanently tightens all downstream checks. Since the accumulator has no decrement, recovery requires external intervention (parameter top-up, governance re-calibration) which may not exist in the design. Key risk factors that create overcount: (1) multi-source aggregation where the sources can report double-counted state, (2) rate-based computation using current parameters rather than per-interval parameters, (3) cross-chain message reordering that produces different final state than source-chain intent, (4) lifecycle transitions (kill/revive) that leave stale indexes enabling retroactive grants.

entries.auditor_notes: Confirmed. Team mitigated via boundary-walk integration and activation-time synchronized state mutations.

confirmed_by.context: Confirmed in cross-chain emission-accounting design review
